design_philosophy.core_loop: Build and manage a skyscraper by placing facilities (offices, residences, hotels, retail, restaurants), manage elevator traffic, fulfill tenant demands, and progress the tower's star rating from 1 to 5 stars, culminating in 'Tower' designation
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing tenant satisfaction against resource constraints and time pressure; elevator management bottlenecks create constant optimization challenges
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a visionary tower developer: design and operate an iconic skyscraper from ground up, watching it evolve into a thriving vertical city with diverse tenants and services
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: The game is fundamentally about elevator logistics and traffic flow as the core design constraint. It refuses to simplify building management—tenant stress directly stems from poor transportation infrastructure. The design philosophy centers on emergent complexity from a single system (elevator simulation) rather than traditional resource tycoon mechanics. Open-ended progression with no fail state reflects designer Yoot Saito's vision that construction simulation should emphasize exploration and discovery over punishment.

difficulty.checkpointing: Continuous save; no permanent failure states. Random events (bombs, VIP visits) create temporary pressure moments but do not end the game
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player has significant agency. Game offers open-ended progression without fail conditions. Difficulty emerges organically from self-imposed goals (reaching 5-star status or 'Tower' designation). Random events can damage the tower but are recoverable. Pacing controlled by in-game time passage, which reviewers noted felt slow—a crucial design choice since income generation requires time to pass

progression.upgrade_system: Star rating system (1-star to 5-star to ultimate 'Tower' designation). New facility types unlock as star rating increases. Physical expansion: maximum 100 floors above ground, 9 floors below ground. Elevator types (standard: 30 floors max; express: full height) scale with tower development
progression.unlock_mechanism: VIP satisfaction and building specific facility types unlock next star level. Final 'Tower' designation requires: 5-star rating + cathedral at top + all possible above-ground floors developed. Underground excavation unlocks randomly when player builds below ground (gold treasure events)
replayability_hooks: Multiple tower configurations with different facility mixes
Random events ensure each playthrough differs (bomb threats, VIP visits, treasure discovery timing)
Challenge of optimizing elevator networks for different tower layouts
Seasonal Easter eggs (Santa Claus fly-by at year-end) encourage extended play

world_building_techniques: Emergent storytelling through tenant behavior and facility satisfaction
Random events narrated via phone calls and notifications (terrorists demanding ransom, VIP visit announcements)
Visual cross-section perspective shows tower ecosystem at a glance; facility types and tenant distribution tell implicit stories
Seasonal events (Santa Claus) create narrative texture without explicit plot
Tower star rating serves as implicit progression narrative—each level represents tower prestige growth

faction_indicators: VIP visitors as status markers for tower prestige
Retail stores vs. restaurants vs. offices as facility classes with distinct clientele
danger_signals: Terrorist bomb threats requiring ransom payment or defusal
Mass tenant evacuation if stress remains unmanaged
Tower damage from uncaught bomb detonations
environmental_storytelling: Physical tower layout reveals design philosophy (elevator placement indicates congestion management priorities)
Facility clustering patterns show player's zoning strategy
Empty vs. occupied floors signal tower health
